entries->death.hitbox.null: %s died from encountering a lethal object
entries->death.jet_exhaust.null: %s was burnt to a crisp by a jet engine
entries->death.jet_exhaust.player: %s was burnt to a crisp by %s's jet engine
entries->death.jet_intake.null: %s was sucked into a jet engine
entries->gui.config.controls.title: Choose a configuration to change:
entries->gui.config.joystick.calibrate1: Move axis until numbers stop changing.
entries->gui.config.joystick.calibrate2: Then hit confirm to save settings.
entries->gui.config.joystick.choosemap: Choose what gets mapped to this button.
entries->gui.config.joystick.disabled: ERROR: The control system locked up when MTS tried to initialize it.  This is likely a driver issue, and happens for some reason on some machines.  The root cause is not known, but if you find out how to make this erorr go away, let us know!  For now, joysick support will be disabled.
entries->gui.config.joystick.error: ERROR: MTS could not initialize the control system!  This is likely due to a device mis-representing itself as a joystick to Java.  Mice have been known to have this issue, especially Corsair mice, so try a different mouse.  You may also try un-plugging your mouse, and then opening this GUI to re-poll the joysticks.  This won't make controls work on boot, but can be used if you only have one mouse.
entries->gui.config.joystick.name: Name:
entries->gui.config.rendering.label: Rendering mode. Slower modes have more compatibility with shaders and performance mods at the cost of FPS.
entries->gui.packmissing.text: MTS has detected that it has been started without a content pack.  This will result in NO vehicles being present!  If you do NOT have a content pack yet installed, please see the Curse page for this mod for a link to one.  If you DID download a pack ensure that it is the same place as all your other mods.  If the pack is in that location, and you are still seeing this message, ensure you are running the correct pack version.
entries->gui.packmissing.title: !ERROR!
entries->interact.battery.chargerlow: This charger does not yet have enough power to charge this battery.
entries->interact.battery.empty: This battery is empty and cannot charge this vehicle.
entries->interact.battery.noengine: There is no engine in this vehicle.  Removing engines from vehicles resets their charge, so charging this vehicle would do nothing!
entries->interact.battery.success: Added 1000mj of charge to this vehicle.
entries->interact.battery.toofull: This vehicle is already fully charged.  You cannot charge it any more.
entries->interact.battery.wrongengines: The engines in this vehicle are not electric and therefore the battery cannot charge them.
entries->interact.fuelhose.alreadylinked: This tank is already linked to another tank and cannot be linked.
entries->interact.fuelhose.differentflui: The source and destination tanks do not contain the same fluid and cannot be linked.
entries->interact.fuelhose.firstlink: Source tank linked to fuel hose.
entries->interact.fuelhose.linkdropped: The linked tanks have moved too far apart from one another.  Breaking link.
entries->interact.fuelhose.toofar: The two tanks are too far away.  They should be no further than 16 blocks apart.
entries->interact.fuelpump.complete: Paid fuel has been dispensed, disconnecting.
entries->interact.fuelpump.fullitems: This pump is set up to require payment for fuel, but it cannot accept more items.  Contact the owner to empty it!
entries->interact.fuelpump.noengine: There is no engine in this vehicle.  Engines with different fuel types will reset fuel, so put an engine in first to avoid fuel loss!
entries->interact.fuelpump.nofuel: The pump is empty.  Either insert fuel into the pump via buckets, or connect it to a fluid or electrical system from the bottom.
entries->interact.fuelpump.toofar: The pump is too far away from the vehicle.  The pump should be no more than 16 blocks away.
entries->interact.fuelpump.wrongengines: The contents in this pump is not a valid fuel for the engines in the vehicle.  Check the manual for details.
entries->interact.fuelpump.wrongtype: This pump does not contain the same fluid as what is in the vehicle.  %s is in the vehicle.
entries->interact.jerrycan.empty: This jerrycan is empty and cannot fuel this vehicle.
entries->interact.jerrycan.noengine: There is no engine in this vehicle.  Removing engines from vehicles resets their fuel tank, so fueling this vehicle would do nothing!
entries->interact.jerrycan.success: Added 1000mb of fluid to this vehicle.
entries->interact.jerrycan.toofull: This vehicle is already full of fuel.  You cannot add any more.
entries->interact.jerrycan.wrongengines: The contents in this jerrycan is not a valid fuel for the engines in the vehicle.  Check the manual for details.
entries->interact.jerrycan.wrongtype: This jerrycan does not contain the same liquid as what is in the vehicle.
entries->interact.jumpercable.alreadylin: This engine is already linked to another engine and cannot be linked.
entries->interact.jumpercable.firstlink: Linking first engine to jumper cable.
entries->interact.jumpercable.linkdroppe: The two engines have moved too far apart from one another.  Breaking link.
entries->interact.jumpercable.powerequal: The two engines now have the same battery level.  Breaking link.
entries->interact.jumpercable.samevehicl: The two engines are on the same vehicle.  That's not going to work well..
entries->interact.jumpercable.toofar: The two engines are too far away.  They should be no further than 16 blocks apart.
entries->interact.key.bind: Key bound to vehicle.  Ready for locking/unlocking use.
entries->interact.key.haskey: This vehicle already has a key for it.  Only OPs may make new keys for vehicles that already have them.
entries->interact.key.wrongkey: This key does not go to this vehicle!
entries->interact.partremove.hasparts: You must first remove all parts from this part to remove it!
entries->interact.partremove.inventory: You cannot remove this part because it is not empty!
entries->interact.partremove.rider: You cannot remove this seat while it is being ridden!
entries->interact.partremove.screwdriver: This part requires a screwdriver to remove!
entries->interact.partremove.wrench: This part requires a wrench to remove!
entries->interact.repair.fail: This thing is totaled and cannot be repaired with this repair pack.  Only special repair packs can repair totaled things.
entries->interact.repair.noneed: This thing is already at full health and doesn't need to be repaired.
entries->interact.repair.pass: Repaired HP by %s.  HP is now at %s of %s.
entries->interact.repair.toosoon: You cannot repair this now.  Wait 10 seconds between repairs.
entries->interact.repair.wrongtype: This repair item does not work on this thing.
entries->interact.road.alreadyconnected: This road segment is already connected to another road.
entries->interact.road.blocked: No space found to place the master block for this road.  Try a less crowded area?
entries->interact.road.blockingblocks: There are blocks blocking the placement of this road.  Break the highlighted red ones, and then click the blue master road block to continue.
entries->interact.road.lanemismatchfirst: The number of lanes at the road segment clicked first does not match the number of lanes for this road.
entries->interact.road.lanemismatchsecon: The number of lanes at the road segment just clicked does not match the number of lanes for this road.
entries->interact.road.same: Both the first point and the second point clicked are the same block.  Reset and try again.
entries->interact.road.toofar: The two points clicked are too far apart.  Either find closer points, or increase the distance in the config file.
entries->interact.trailer.alreadytowed: The only nearby vehicle is already being towed.
entries->interact.trailer.feedbackloop: The only nearby vehicles are being towed by this vehicle, towing would result in an infinite towing loop.
entries->interact.trailer.mismatch: A hitch was found that is close enough to connect, but was not the correct type.  Also, a matching hitch was found, but it was too far to connect.  Are you trying to use the wrong hitch?
entries->interact.trailer.notfound: No trailers were found anywhere near this vehicle.
entries->interact.trailer.toofar: The trailer is too far from the vehicle.
entries->interact.trailer.wronghitch: This vehicle does not have the correct hitch for this trailer.
entries->interact.vehicle.needkey: You must have the key of this vehicle to start it!
entries->interact.vehicle.nofuel: There is no fuel in this vehicle.  The engine cannot start!
entries->interact.vehicle.nospace: There is not enough space to place down this vehicle here.
entries->iteminfo.battery.drain: Charge a vehicle by right-clicking with a full charge.
entries->iteminfo.battery.fill: Chargethis battery by right-clicking a charger with power.
entries->iteminfo.fuelhose:
Use on a vehicle tank to select.
Use on second tank or vehicle to link.
Hose will transfer liquids from tank 1 to selected.
Hose will stop when tank 1 is empty, selected is full or if tank 1 and selected are too far away.
entries->iteminfo.jerrycan.drain: Fill a vehicle by right-clicking with a full can.
entries->iteminfo.jerrycan.fill: Fill this can by right-clicking a fuel pump.
entries->iteminfo.jumpercables:
Use on an engine to select.
Use on second engine to link.
Linked engines will exchange power.
Engines will un-link when power is even or if they get too far away.
entries->iteminfo.jumperpack: Use on a vehicle to charge battery to max level.
entries->iteminfo.key: Sneak-use on cars to take ownership, right-click to lock/unlock.
entries->iteminfo.paintgun:
Use on a vehicle, a part, decor, or sign to change its color.
Note: Not all things you can click will have multiple colors.
entries->iteminfo.repairpack.engine: This repair pack also removes all engine hours.
entries->iteminfo.repairpack.part: Use on a part to repair it.  This pack heals the part:
entries->iteminfo.repairpack.untotal: This repair pack is able to repair totaled vehicles as well.
entries->iteminfo.repairpack.vehicle: Use on a vehicle to repair it.  This pack heals the vehicle:
entries->iteminfo.scanner:
Hover over a part box to see what can be placed at that slot.
Sneak while using to cycle slower.
entries->iteminfo.screwdriver: Like a wrench, but used only to remove specific parts that wrenches cannot.  Does not interact with blocks or other things.
entries->iteminfo.ticket:
Right-click on a vehicle to load nearby entities.
Right-click on a seat to load just that seat.
Sneak-click on a vehicle to unload all entities.
Note: Will not unload players.
entries->iteminfo.wrench:
Attack a vehicle part to remove it.
Sneak-attack a vehicle to remove entire vehicle.
Use on vehicle to change instruments.
Sneak-use on vehicle or blocks to change text.
Use on pole components to remove them.
Use on fuel pumps you placed to change prices.
entries->iteminfo.y2kbutton:
Use in a world to turn off all vehicle engines, engage their parking brakes, and turn off their lights.  Sneak-use to dump all vehicle positions in the chat.  
Only usable by admins.

entries->mts.gunbench.name: Gun Bench
Thank you for downloading Immersive Vehicles!  This manual goes over how to construct and operate all the vehicles in the mod. It is STRONGLY suggested new players read this to get familiar with the various systems. Failing to do so and asking a question on the Discord that's covered in this manual is frowned upon and will just get you made fun of, so make sure you read all the way through!

Note that aircraft have their own manual as flying them is more complex than just driving a car, so see that one for details. If there's something that is not included in this manual for vehicles, this is the time when you ask on Discord. We'll be happy to answer your question and add it to the manual in a later update!  Oh, and you can scroll through this manual (as well as all GUIs) with your mouse wheel, if your mouse is equipped with one.
entries->mts.handbook_car.booklet.page10: No matter what they are in, engines form the heart of your vehicle. Engines come in a variety of fuel consumptions and max RPMs, so choose carefully based on your application. Fuel-less versions of engines are available in the creative tab, should you not wish to mess with fuel.

Beware: engines will gradually accumulate hours and lose their power, going so far as to start backfiring when really worn out. Over-revving, over-heating, and running the engine too fast before it has warmed up will quickly add hours to an otherwise new engine. Engines may also accumulate hours from damage, going so far as to spring leaks or lose their electric starters.
entries->mts.handbook_car.booklet.page11: Instruments make piloting a plane and driving a car much more immersive. They are also useful for monitoring key properties like fuel levels, RPM, and engine temps, and make flying in the dark much less hazardous. While some vehicles come with instruments when first placed down, most do not.

Instruments are added to vehicles by right-clicking with the wrench on them. The resulting GUI shows a view of the HUD and all instruments in your possession that work on that vehicle. Simply click the slot you want to put the instrument in, and click that instrument.
entries->mts.handbook_car.booklet.page12: The only thing worse than getting your nice car blown up by a creeper is having it (or parts of it) stolen by other players. Normally, vehicles may only be modified by their owners (the person who last placed them), but can be driven by anyone. By using a key you can lock vehicles, ensuring nobody can drive off with, or take things out of them.

Locked vehicles may not be entered, and their inventories may not be accessed, except by players inside them. This allows you to lock yourself into vehicles to prevent random passengers. If a player that isn't the vehicle's owner has a key, they may enter and drive it, but they still cannot modify it. Note that OPs bypass these restrictions to allow server admins to move/remove abandoned vehicles. You can also tow vehicles you're not the owner of. Wink wink.
entries->mts.handbook_car.booklet.page13: While roads aren't vehicles, they are something all vehicles will interact with. There are two types of roads in the mod: static and dynamic. Static roads are pre-built sections that you place down like a Lego road plate. Dynamic roads are the opposite: flexible connections you can put anywhere.

For dynamic roads, you need to define two points to make a road path. The first is done by sneak-clicking a block in the direction for the road to go. The second is done by moving to the other end of where you want the road, and face in the direction towards the first point. You can also click an existing road segment rather than a block, and the road will auto-connect to that segment. This includes static sections, which are how you make road junctions.
entries->mts.handbook_car.booklet.page14: IV offers multiple control schemes. The standard scheme is the keyboard control. Unlike other mods, IV is equipped with a no-bind system. This prevents it from clogging up keybindings and from intercepting and taking other mod's keystrokes. Configuration for this mode, and the other control modes, can be activated by pressing the config key (P by default).

This key is the only one that's bound in the standard way in the controls menu, and may be double-bound by default in some modpacks, so if you can't bring up the config menu check your keybindings. Do note that while IV will not block other mod's key signals, it may still be affected by the signals of other mods who do not use context-sensitive triggers. Keep this in mind when controlling vehicles.
entries->mts.handbook_car.booklet.page15: The second control scheme is a Joystick-Keyboard hybrid. This controller MUST be connected at the time you start Minecraft. Configuration is done through the JOYSTICK section of the P-key menu. When an axis such as yaw or pitch is being controlled by a joystick it will be unavailable on the keyboard. This is the case even if you un-plug the joystick, so un-bind it before you do.

The final method of control available is a mouse-yoke system similar to both Flan's and MCHeli. For all of these controls, there are a few extra settings in the P-key CONFIG menu. These can be used to control joystick dead-zone, sensitivity, keyboard cooldown, as well as other general control settings.
entries->mts.handbook_car.booklet.page16: Driving a car (or a boat) is so simple, even a villager could do it. The default controls use the simple throttle setup, where W makes the vehicle move forwards, and S stops it and then makes it move backwards. A and D steer left and right. Some vehicles are equipped with lights, horns, sirens, etc., so check your controls and the panel (U key) for details.

If you want a more realistic driving experience, you can turn off simple throttle. This will make all cars start in neutral, and pressing R puts them into forwards. Pressing F will shift back into neutral, and F again will shift into reverse. In this mode, W is the gas, and S is the brake. Shifting should be done while stopped to avoid scary transmission noises. The only exception are engines with manual transmissions, which have multiple forwards gears.
entries->mts.handbook_car.booklet.page17: IV has many config options, some of which aren't available in the in-game config menu. See the mtsconfig.json file in your config directory for a full listing and description of configs. Commonly-modified configs include block breakage, fuel consumption, and fuel values.

Should you wish to modify the recipes for the items made on the benches, you may do so by adding an override in the mtscraftingoverrides.json file. See that file for details on how to do so. Anything created on a crafting bench follows the normal process for editing, e.g. CraftTweaker.
Like most mods that have packs and lots of content, IV uses crafting benches for crafting. The Vehicle Bench is used to craft vehicles, while the other benches craft their own parts. You'll likely want to start filling your garage early with these benches as most vehicles use at least one type of every part. JEI is supported, and will tell you what benches make what parts.

Crafting the benches themselves is as simple as making them in a vanilla crafting table. JEI or your mod of choice will show you these recipes. The first benches you should craft are the Component Bench and the Decor Bench. These are used by a lot of packs to add custom items that are used to craft vehicle parts, and even vehicles themselves. Some packs also use the Custom Bench to make parts for other parts, so that one is also worth a look.
Assembling vehicles is as simple as clicking them with the required parts. When holding a valid part a green hologram will appear in locations where you can place it. Simply right-click with that part and it will attach to the vehicle. Note that some parts may require other parts to be installed before they may be placed, such as propellers that connect to aircraft engines.

If you are holding a part and the hologram is red, it means the part is the correct type, but the wrong size. One common example is a wheel that is too big or too small for the vehicle. Another is if a pack creator wants to restrict that location to specific parts, such as a specific engine, so try parts from the pack first. Creators may also put what goes to the vehicle in the item description.
If you know a part should go on a vehicle, but can't figure out which part this may be, then the Scanner may be of use to you. It will show all part positions, and cycle through all possible parts that can go at the pointed-at position. If nothing cycles, and you don't hear beeping, then the slot cannot take anything. This should be reported to the pack author as it indicates an invalid slot.

If you want to remove a part, simply attack it with a wrench. This may only be done on parts that don't have other parts on them. i.e. you can remove a tire, but if it has a hubcap on it, you need to remove the hubcap first before you can remove the tire itself. Note that some parts, such as those that come with the vehicle, may be permanent and cannot be removed. If you want to remove the vehicle itself from the world, sneak-attack it with the wrench.
While vehicles are spawned in the color chosen when crafting, you may change this color by using the paint gun. This will let you change liveries for the vehicle. Do note that some vehicles may restrict some parts to a specific livery, such as police cars to the police livery.

Parts can be changed with the paint gun as well, though most have only a single color option. Some vehicles support 'toned' paint schemes. These result in parts being changed to match the vehicle color. Keep this in mind when using the paint gun. Also note that clicking the vehicle paints the vehicle (and toned parts), whereas clicking a part paints that part only.
Normally, a good driver doesn't crash. Should you not be a good driver, one of a few things may happen. Normally, the block you hit will break and you will slow down. However, this may not happen if the block is very hard (obsidian, bedrock, etc.), or if block breaking is disabled in the config.

If you hit enough blocks at a high enough speed, the vehicle will crash and blow up. The explosion size being determined on the amount of fuel in the vehicle. Some crafting materials and all parts will be dropped, but these may be destroyed in the explosion. Note that both the explosion and the destruction of the vehicle itself are configs and may be disabled if desired.
Note that blowing up a vehicle by running it into a wall is not the only way to ruin a perfectly good car.  You can also take damage from mobs, projectiles, guns, and other various sources.  If you take more than the vehicle can stand, it will stop working.  Vehicle health is displayed while riding a vehicle as the driver in the overlay.

To repair a vehicle, you can either craft a repair item and use it, or repair the vehicle at the vehicle bench.  Repair items are pack-specific, and benches have to be set up with repair recipes to function, so contact the author of the pack you are using if you find yourself without repair options.
Vehicles require fuel (though some come pre-fueled), and are fueled from a fuel pump. Just click the fuel pump and it will automatically connect to the nearest vehicle. The pump will then transfer fuel at a rate of 10 mb/t. The pump itself has a 15000mb reservoir and can be filled directly from liquid-holding containers, or by connecting a pipe to the bottom from pipe systems compatible with Forge fluids.

Note that if you connect a pipe to the bottom of the pump you can set an item-based cost for using the pump. Simply put the item stack you wish to charge in your inventory, then click the pump with a wrench to open the price GUI. From there you can click a slot to set the item and the amount of fuel that item will allow. This may only be done by the person who placed the pump, or a server admin.
The potency values, as well as what fluids work with what engine fuel types, are all configurable in the config file. In addition, there exists a config option called fuelUsageFactor that globally sets the fuel consumption for all engines. If you find engines to be thirstier than you'd like, you can adjust that rather than adjusting each fuel individually.

Each engine has a fuel type that is associated with it. Fuel types can be served by multiple fluids, but each fluid's potency will likely differ. Potency is how much energy that fluid has, with higher-potency fluids lasting longer in vehicles. Valid fluids for each engine and the potencies are displayed in the engine item tooltip.
entries->mts.handbook_car.name: IV Operator's Manual (READ ME!)
entries->mts.handbook_plane.booklet.page: Propellers

If you're going to use anything that's not a car, it will likely need one of these (well, unless it's a jet). Larger diameter propellers provide more thrust than smaller ones; however, larger-diameter propellers also need larger engines to turn, so it may be that a weak engine can produce more thrust with a smaller propeller than a larger one. Propellers are created on the Propeller Bench.

Propeller pitch is also important, perhaps more so than diameter, as it determines the optimal speed at any RPM. Anything below this speed will speed the aircraft up, anything above will slow it down. Note that propellers that have dynamic pitch will adjust their pitch in flight to maintain optimum thrust, and may also be reversed by pressing the REV button in the plane panel. Reversed propellers are not very effective, so make sure to use plenty of throttle and watch your temps!
Propellers
Time to Fly!

Before your first flight you should be aware of a few key facts. First, planes do not go up when you pull up. We repeat: PLANES DO NOT GO UP WHEN YOU PULL UP!  This is the #1 cause of crashes for new pilots. In MFS, and in real-life, planes gain lift through moving air over the wings. Therefore, the proper way to lift off the ground is to go fast so lots of air moves over the wings and makes lots of lift.

This means that you will need a decent runway for takeoff, and it is highly recommended you avoid taking off towards high things like trees and mountains. If you are piloting a plane equipped with flaps you can set a few notches to increase the lifting power of the plane. Note that this will cause a loss in fuel efficiency and slow you down, so only use flaps when performing takeoffs and landings. Also note that weight (items in inventory and cargo) is a factor in plane handling, so make sure not to overload your plane.
Time to Fly!
In-Flight

Once your plane is up in the air flying just becomes a matter of two things: not crashing into wild mountains, and not crashing into the ground. The former can simply be dodged by banking the plane into a turn by rolling the airplane (A and D) or simply flying above build height. Turns will slowly decrease your altitude, so watch that if you're close to terrain.

While flying you may have to keep holding inputs to make your plane behave the way you want. If you keep climbing, it's because you have too much thrust and should let off the throttle. You can also add some trim and keep the throttle up to get a bit more speed at the cost of fuel economy. You may also move to other seats while flying by right-clicking them, and some planes even allow for multiple pilots. Check the Vehicle Bench for specifics on seat and pilot counts.
In-Flight
Using Beacons

Beacons (from this mod, not Vanilla ones) are used to define waypoints that can be used with the instruments on some planes. Placing down a beacon and naming it will let you select it in the U-panel. A selected beacon is valid if it is green, and red if it doesn't exist. Once a beacon is selected, all instruments will perform their functions using it.

There are two instruments that use beacons. The ADF simply points towards the selected beacon, but the ILS is a bit different. It uses the beacon's bearing and glideslope to help you line up landing. Glideslope is the landing angle, and bearing is the landing heading. The two bars move in the ILS depending on how far you are 'off' from the beacon, with centered bars meaning you're on the landing beam.
Using Beacons
Trim/Autopilot

Trim is normally overlooked by most pilots, but is a useful tool to make flying much easier. Trim can be adjusted in the U-panel, with each trim doing different things. Elevator trim makes for a constant pitch, which is useful if you keep climbing because you want to go faster. Yaw is useful if you lose an engine and keep yawing.

While you can manually apply trim, you can also let the autopilot do it for you (if the aircraft is equipped with it). Autopilot will apply elevator trim to keep the altitude the same, and will apply aileron trim to keep the aircraft level. For helicopters, it will function as an auto-hover, keeping the heli in the same place and same altitude.
Trim/Autopilot
Landing

There is a fine line between successfully landing a plane and a controlled crash. To avoid that second category it is important to remember the saying, 'To go down you need to slow down.'  Speed is what keeps your plane aloft, so a reduction in speed is needed to land. This can be as simple as lowering the throttle and applying the flaps.

Some times, however, you end up in that second category. Running out of fuel is the most common way to get down quickly, but it is not necessarily deadly. Planes can glide without engines, giving you a short amount of time to pick a flat spot to become an impromptu runway. Should one not be available a body of water is also a good choice as it will prevent explosions on a hard crash and will allow you to bail while in mid-air. Anything hilly should be avoided at all costs.
Landing

entries->sytstem.soundslot: IMMERSIVE VEHICLES ERROR: Tried to play a sound, but was told no sound slots were available. Some mod is taking up all the slots. If you have Immersive Railroading, set override sound channels to false in that mod's config. If running GregTech, set maxNumSounds to a lower value in that mod's config. If you have Receiver Gun Mod, un-install it (there is no config for this incopatibility).  If you have fixRTM, set expandPlayableSoundSlotCount to false.  Dynamic Surrondings and Optifine also may cause issues. Apply fixes, or complain to those mod's authors.
entries->sytstem.soundsystem: IMMERSIVE VEHICLES ERROR: Tried to play a sound but couldn't due to an audio system fault.  Do you have bad audio drivers?
